did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) utilizing machine learning algorithms and deep features to retrieve visually similar dermoscopy images for skin lesion classification (Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma). The system employed a classifier trained on these four classes to compute cosine distances and retrieve similar images, which were then used to evaluate user classification accuracy and confidence in a dermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a user study involving 16 novice users to evaluate the impact of a Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) system on classification accuracy and confidence. It includes specific methodology (within-subjects design, dataset description, system implementation), quantitative results (accuracy rates, p-values from t-tests), and discussion of findings, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: How Users Perceive Content-Based Image Retrieval for Identifying Skin Images
brief_summary: This study evaluated the impact of a Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) system on the classification accuracy and confidence of 16 novice users identifying four common skin lesion categories: Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma. Utilizing dermoscopy images from the ISIC archive and a dermoscopy atlas, the researchers employed a within-subjects experimental design where participants classified images with and without CBIR assistance. The CBIR system retrieved visually similar images based on cosine distances computed from deep features of a machine learning classifier. Results showed that using CBIR significantly improved mean classification accuracy from 51.56% to 60.94% and increased user confidence levels (P < 0.05). The findings suggest that CBIR can effectively support non-experts in dermatological diagnosis, although trust in the system did not significantly increase.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four common skin lesion categories used in the study, selected from the ISIC archive and dermoscopy atlas. 5 query images per condition were selected from this category.
One of the four common skin lesion categories used in the study. The study noted that accuracy for this category actually decreased with CBIR, possibly due to limited representation in the dataset.
One of the four common skin lesion categories used in the study. Mentioned in the introduction as a type of skin cancer needing early detection.
One of the four common skin lesion categories used in the study. Described in the introduction as the deadliest type of skin cancer. The improvement in accuracy was greatest for this category.

all_task_types.target: Four discrete skin lesion categories: Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma.
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates a Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) system that utilizes a machine learning classifier trained on four classes to compute cosine distances and retrieve visually similar images. The primary task assessed is the user's ability to classify the query image into one of these four discrete diagnostic categories. The system uses classification probabilities to compute features for retrieval, but the ultimate output and evaluation metric focus on discrete class prediction accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from The International Skin Imaging Collaboration used as a source for dermoscopy images.

use.training: Images from this dataset were part of the pool from which the study's dataset was constructed; the specific classifier used to compute deep features was trained on these 4 classes (reference [11] suggests deep classification features were used).
use.testing: Images from this dataset were used as query images in the user study.
use.other: Served as a primary source for the 1021 images in the study's dataset.

datasets_used.name: Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopy atlas (Book and CD-ROM) used as a source for dermoscopy images.

use.training: Images from this atlas were part of the pool from which the study's dataset was constructed.
use.testing: Images from this atlas were used as query images in the user study.
use.other: Served as a primary source for the 1021 images in the study's dataset.

datasets_used.name: Study Dataset (Custom Collection)
datasets_used.description: A curated collection of 1021 dermoscopy images approved by an expert dermatologist, sourced from the ISIC archive and the dermoscopy atlas. It was limited to 4 common skin lesion categories.

use.training: Used to build the reference collection for the CBIR system; deep features were extracted from these images to compute cosine distances.
use.testing: 40 query images were selected from this dataset (20 for the 'without CBIR' condition and 20 for the 'with CBIR' condition) for the user study. Among these, 36 were unique query images.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: P-value (from two-tailed t-test for paired samples)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To test the null hypothesis that there is no difference in the means of classification accuracy between the conditions (without CBIR vs. with CBIR).
To test the null hypothesis that there is no difference in the means of user confidence levels between the conditions (without CBIR vs. with CBIR).
To test the null hypothesis that there is no difference in the means of user trust levels between pre-task and post-task questionnaires.
To determine statistical significance for the t-tests, using P < 0.05.
To report the variability of trust scores reported by users.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the means of the measured variable (classification accuracy, user confidence, or trust) between the conditions (without CBIR vs. with CBIR, or pre-task vs. post-task).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in the means of the measured variable between the conditions.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using a threshold of P < 0.05. The study found significant improvements in mean classification accuracy (P < 0.05) and mean user confidence (P < 0.05) when using CBIR compared to not using it. However, the difference in user trust levels between pre-task and post-task questionnaires was not significant (p = 0.65).

Study Dataset (Custom Collection - 1021.notes: The improvement in mean classification accuracy from 51.56% to 60.94% was statistically significant (P < 0.05). The improvement was greatest for Nevus and Malignant Melanoma. For Seborrheic Keratosis, accuracy decreased, but the difference was not significant.

Study Dataset (Custom Collection - User.notes: The overall mean user confidence score increased from 3.47 without CBIR to 3.7 with CBIR (P < 0.05). Users' mean confidence in True Positive (TP) cases improved by 6.59% (P < 0.05). When classification was incorrect, confidence increased by only 2.52%. There was a significant difference between confidence on correct (78.16%) and incorrect (69.74%) classifications using CBIR (P < 0.05).
The difference in trust levels between pre-task and post-task questionnaires was not significant (p = 0.65).

architecture_used.main_model: Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) system utilizing a machine learning classifier for deep feature extraction
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; the system used an 'existing classifier' trained on four classes (Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Malignant Melanoma) to compute deep features. Reference [11] (Tschandl et al., 2018) is cited for the diagnostic accuracy of content-based dermatoscopic image retrieval with deep classification features.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Deep features extracted from the query image and compared against a database of 1021 images.
architecture_details.retrieval_metric: Cosine distance computed between the deep feature vector of the query image and the deep feature vectors of images in the database.
architecture_details.retrieval_count: Top 20 closest images were stored in a dictionary; the 15 most visually similar images were returned to the user interface.
architecture_details.implementation: User interface software written using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, NodeJS, and MongoDB.

training_details.training_data: Four classes of skin lesion images from the ISIC archive and Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy.

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: The system did not output a classification directly to the user but retrieved visually similar images based on classification probabilities/deep features from the underlying classifier.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: machine learning classifier (trained on four classes: Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma)
conclusions: Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) significantly improved the mean classification accuracy of novice users from 51.56% to 60.94% when identifying four common skin lesion categories (Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma).
The use of CBIR significantly increased user confidence levels (P < 0.05), with the greatest improvement observed in True Positive cases.
The improvement in classification accuracy was most pronounced for Nevus and Malignant Melanoma categories.
Classification accuracy for Seborrheic Keratosis decreased with CBIR assistance, which the authors attribute to limited representation of this category in the dataset.

User trust in the CBIR system did not significantly increase after using the tool compared to pre-task trust levels (p = 0.65), potentially due to the novelty of the system and the users' lack of medical expertise.
CBIR serves as a transparent and explainable artificial intelligence application that can support non-experts in dermatological diagnosis by providing visually similar reference images.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the use of small, public datasets with potentially low-quality images and a limited number of Seborrheic Keratosis images, suggesting that system accuracy may improve with larger, more balanced databases.

The study was limited to novice users (graduate students) as proxies for medical students and general physicians, and future work is needed to determine if these results transfer to expert dermatologists.
important_remarks: The study population consisted exclusively of 16 novice users (graduate students) as proxies for medical students and general physicians; results may not transfer to expert dermatologists or primary care physicians with different baseline knowledge.

Classification accuracy for Seborrheic Keratosis decreased with CBIR assistance (from 36.25% to 23.75%), which the authors attribute to limited representation of this category in the dataset, highlighting a potential bias or limitation in the reference database.

The study was limited to small, public datasets (ISIC archive and Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy) which often contain low-quality images; system accuracy may improve with larger, more balanced databases.

User trust in the CBIR system did not significantly increase after using the tool compared to pre-task trust levels (p = 0.65), potentially due to the novelty of the system and the users' lack of medical expertise.

The dataset was limited to four common skin lesion categories (Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma), restricting the generalizability of findings to other dermatological conditions.
The study utilized a within-subjects design with randomization to mitigate fatigue and learning effects, but the small sample size (N=16) limits statistical power and external validity.

The CBIR system relied on an existing classifier trained on four classes to compute deep features and cosine distances; specific details of the underlying classifier architecture were not reported in this paper (referenced as [11]).
The study did not report the distribution of image quality or specific artifacts (e.g., ruler, hair) in the query images, which are noted as major decision-making challenges in the discussion.
